speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This old photo was taken on one of the occasions when I went to Israel to see my sister. And we were both in-- in Tel Aviv, and we decided we would go into a photographer and have a picture taken, just on the spur of the moment. We were just walking, doing some window shopping and so on, and this is the picture that was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Roughly which year? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Tel Aviv in 1958, when we were just going for a walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken when I got married to Peter Jay, and that was taken in front of the synagogue, of Walm Lane Synagogue in Cricklewood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1976. June 1976. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo was taken when my sister came to see me because I was very, very ill actually. I-- I had a pacemaker, which we never actually mentioned. But anyway, and my sister came especially to stay with me. And this is, we were all together with my children and grandchildren, and my sister sits right next to me. This picture was taken in my-- in my flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who is  who on the photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have got I am in the middle in the brown. My sister is in blue next to me. Then there is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Denise is to the right. Denise is to the right. There is my-- Maya, my granddaughter, my son's daughter. Then there's Aron next. Then there's Julien, my son's-- my grandson, my son's son. Then there is Doniel, my other grandson, and [? Eliasa, ?] my son-in-law, and my son, Michael, and his wife, Diane. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe the photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken when my sister came to-- to London to stay with me for a while whilst I wasn't feeling well. In the middle, right in the center is me, holding the baby, my-- my youngest granddaughter. Next to me is my sister, to the left. To the right is my daughter Denise. Above her is my daughter-in-law, Diane, and my son. Michael, and my son-in-law, Ellie, and his son Doniel. Then comes Julien [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Sharon. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and Aron and Maya Laura. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, can you please describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken at our reunion. It's an annual reunion, once a year when it comes to the liberation of the war, which is usually in May. And here I am lighting one of the candles for the six million Jews that perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reunion of whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a reunion of our boys and girls that we came over to this country, that we were-- came over to England, that we were liberated. And once a year we have a reunion where we all get together and we make a-- a big do out of it. We have dinner and dance. And-- and-- but of course, it is also memorial to all of us, and we light the candles of the six million Jews that died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in one of the halls in-- in London. I think this one was taken in-- in King-- the King David Hotel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in London. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken at one of the reunions when they took a picture of all the girls. There are wives of the survivors of the boys, and there is me and other girls, survivors. I am sitting here with the chain in the middle, which I've got a white pearl chain and a dark dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 12, 13 years ago. I'm not sure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this photo please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This photo was taken when I arrived in England in Windermere. And this photo is actually in the book from Sir Martin Gilbert.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the title of the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: The title of the book is And since I am-- we were more boys, only a few girls, then I was always called one of the boys. And here is the picture of me, of one of the boys. And the previous picture was also taken from a Sir Martin Gilbert book, which is a group picture, which I was also on it and all the girls that married to our boys. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Jay, can you please introduce everyone here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is-- these are my wonderful children. Starting from the eldest, Denise, which wears a mauve dress. Then comes her brother, my son Michael. To the left of-- of Denise is my oldest granddaughter, Sharon. And then comes to the right is my-- my other granddaughter, Maya, Maya Laura. Then there is Julien, my grandson. Here is my Aron, my grandson Aron, and Kochawa my youngest granddaughter. And my husband, Peter.

speaker: interviewee
text: You forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Does anyone have to say something regarding the experience of your mother and grandmother? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could say a lot. I feel very bad about the fact that I never met my grandparents, and what happened to them, and to all the rest of the family. And I have very strong feelings against the Germans. I don't care if it's being prejudiced. I wouldn't go to Germany. I never went there. And when I went past to go to Switzerland, I made my husband do a long detour so that we shouldn't go through Germany. I have very strong feelings against Germans. And what else can one say? Thank goodness my mother survived, that we're all here, and we've got children to carry on with. That's all I've got to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say that I don't quite feel the same as my sister, Denise, about the Germans. I feel the-- the generation, the new generation of Germans, you can't blame them for what happened during the Holocaust. But I think that what-- the whole experience of my mother has been an incredible one, an incredible story. And the way she survived is an absolute miracle for which, really, we have to thank God that we're all here today, alive now.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few years ago, I went on the March of the Living. I was very privileged to go, and it was a real great experience for me. And I've really, really learned so much from it. I don't know. It's definitely such an important thing that please God, my children, I'll be able to tell them about it. And it's close to me, so it will be maybe a little bit easier to explain. Because I'm sure as the generations go on, it will become more and more of a distant thing, and more and more of a difficult thing for people to understand, as with other tragedies. So I'm very grateful that I did have the opportunity to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Jay, would you like to add something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can only say that my family, when-- when they-- they were very small, I never liked talking about it, and I didn't tell them much about it. That's why they don't really know a lot. It's recently that they actually got to know more about it than before. Because it was such a traumatic thing for me, I didn't want my-- I wanted to spare the children to have this trauma which I went through. What else can I-- can-- what can I say? Thank God that they turned out to be as they are. They're good children, and I'm very proud of them. And they're Jewish children, and I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, Mrs. Jay, thank you very much for the interview. And thank you. Can you please describe the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in Israel with my wonderful family in Israel. Here, I have got-- I am sitting right next to my sister. My sister is to the left. To the right is my nephew, that is my sister's son. Then there's his wife and my husband. And then there is my-- my-- there is Rachel, my darling niece, with her husband Moshe. To the left of them is Sharon, my granddaughter. She was with us in Israel. Then there is my cousin. She is a-- a survivor from Poland. She is from my mother's side's family from [? Pabianitza ?], but she lived in Lodz before the war. With her lovely daughter Esther and Herman.
